text: July 12, 2004, personal interview with Mr. William Miles. Tell me a little bit about your background please. Okay, I was born in Prince of Anne Maryland, 1933. I am 70 years old. My parents are from the area. They were born and raised in some said county. My father passed away in 1999. My mother is 18 years old and she is doing better than all of us. I am the oldest of 11 brothers and sisters, 7 boys and 4 girls. I have eight masters in math education and my family in university. My mother graduated from the year 1936. They do six years to live in relation to the city. Oh yes, they were running. I mean, there was rumbling. There were students who were rumbling from their inspections. There were students who had platforms that were running for different offices in and around the school. I remember the five that became the state of the world. The fourth one was we were going to integrate all of the facilities. I did not come about doing that. I am a father of three children. One after the college of New York. I was an educator and I did not come to the school. I was a student at the college of New York. I was a student at the college of New York. I was a student at the college of New York. She ended up having her own house to be in the city. She had to give that up because of her own teaching. My hobbies and leisure activities have been tied to sports. I wanted to come in and learn that I had no skills. That allowed me to take professionally and I wanted to coach. I was looking at the skills that I could do about that. I know my skills well. Everything that I could. I was able to get a scholarship in baseball. I was able to play baseball in the Army. I had a lifetime of playing baseball. One of the things I have in the series of questions I have on that is she's going to follow on Easter. Sure, was baseball on the one? Was that the big game? When I was growing up, it was the big game. Primarily because it was easy to get into. Every community had a baseball team when I was growing up. Even there were white baseball teams that were black baseball teams. Every village, every community had a baseball team. When I was growing up in my community was the old school Eagles and Prince of the Ann House. You had teams in De La Llan, you had teams in Vanden. You had teams in Pokemon. You had teams here in Salzburg, Fulton. You had two teams here in Salzburg. There are a lot of them. We would have a schedule of baseball games from each to money. We would usually start a game from one month to then. We would play all the way because they got cold and hot. Of course, our game is your time to the weekend. We played Saturday, Sunday and holidays. Matter of fact, I got married on Saturday, September 10th of the fifth. Which was Saturday before Labor Day. We had a double hit in that Saturday. I missed the first game. You get married when I played the second game. Much to the city of Unibai. We had a double hit in that Sunday. We had a double hit in that Monday. I went to work at 2D. We were playing at that time. I was playing at that time. I did not miss playing professionally. I'd check it out on the coaching. I thought about going into coaching full time seriously. At the word of a time to coach. Integration has started coming into play. The big white school was siphoning all of the good black school. We could see that coming when I was a senior at A&T. I was becoming more difficult for the coaches to get the ball players that they used to add in the back pocket. The interject, you started seeing that I remember reading something about Paul Bryant. He said, when they opened up Alameda, exactly. He got these good ball players there. That were the Norman Bargitriotti and M. They had brought in some old schools there. They could see out 100 ball players. They had 100 ball players that were all first training in many colleges there. They were all going to the black school in the back pocket. When it was time for me to step on the coaching scene. I did not have the foresight to see the opportunity that I will be able to come out. When I see things opening up for my coaching head, make the level of coaching Alameda, the big schools and this kind of thing. I just thought of trying to say, hey, I should have stayed with him. But I don't have any regrets. You're left as you were talking to your life. You've been a vice principal for 10 years and you've retired in 2000. Tell me just a little bit about what you're doing in retirement. I retired in 2000 after 40 years in education. Having taught at the college level at the high school level there. I went into ministry in 1999 and continued in ministry. To the present day, I started my six year in ministry on a rural charge in Marion Station, Maryland. It was a natural step for me and I've always worked with people. I've always been a servant to the people. I thought that being in ministry was an opportunity to give back to the community. Because I had taken so much out of the community before. I had gotten into the very challenging and very rewarding. Sitting there listening to you talk, I was thinking people person. But I think we were teachers too. But giving to the community yet. It's sort of just like a natural transition. There was a natural transition there. In rural areas in many instances, they cannot afford to hire cashers from out of seminary with families. Who need an income beyond the minimum income there. But with my retirement and everything, to supplement the income. The money that I receive is secondary. I think I get back on who I'm more than I receive monetary. We talked to some of the young people. To make money as all of what you've worked all about. And go to the left because you enjoyed it before you joined us. And that's what you would be doing. And again, we enjoy it. As I stayed on my airbush, the purpose of our interview is our commonality and our friendship over the years in baseball. Because you get to go with the background about you in baseball. We sort of jump around talking about sports. But, you know, background brought up today. And you know, the camps and the other ones, you think like that. But I don't think we can. Okay, you must be like that. When I grew up in the 40s and 50s, everything was in this area. When I see this area I'm talking about Maryland. The issue of Maryland in particular, there was a second gate. That was very little formal recreation in terms of participating in your little day. And then you're famous big and the American Legion and all of these kinds of things that have come up now in the kitchen. Participating. Each community had a ball club. And that's it. And the position was on the end. There was a ball club. It was supported by the community. They would take up a collective new church. And by these phones, the by-bats, and everything that we were prepared to deal with together and replicate. Hey, come on, I'm going. You see the women's natural. The individual looks there. The patient is not one here. But we grew up looking forward to playing on the community. And we worshiped the guys who were playing. They were our heroes. They were guys that we looked up to. We respected them in the same manner that kids now respect. Majorly farfares. We probably had a higher regard to them because of the close relationship. We saw them. We knew that they were human beings. We could cut them down. But they stood up and hit our home run 400 feet. Put the talent, was it running at the ball and played like that guy. So in these communities, you had guys looking forward to playing on the community team. The high schools did not have baseball teams. When I was going... What was the most difficult teams of the everybody? The high school, and which I graduated from, had basketball. And they had soccer. There was a soccer in the spring, in the fall. And they had basketball in the winter. And they had track there. Because it was a money thing. They did not take a whole lot to have a basketball team. You get a few uniforms and a basketball. And you have a basketball program. And saying, stop it. You get a couple of balls. And you get out on the field. There. A couple of schools around Southbury, high school and football. And Agamack, high school. And Smith had football and had baseball. You didn't see it. I started baseball after coming out of college in the... I thought someone said, using your high school. They started that way back in 1958. I think it was, yes. I started back at someone said in 1588, 59. And when you spring up in 1989, you started with baseball at some extent. You were in high school. You were in an integrated generation. You were in high school. But getting back to what you were in. One question. Like you said, you leave it from Montgomery County. Wouldn't they integrate the schools here? You said 59 and integrated baseball. And then I'm not, well, said it. But then I'm, well, said it. It would have had to have been after 64. I don't know the exact date. But not in, no, but not in that time. Not to 64. Because I, well, that Joe, some say, that everything has grown from 68 to 62. And then I left. It would have had to have been like, I'd say, almost 66, 67. I won't say that that's for sure, but these schools were integrated. Sometimes doing that time. But when I grew up, it was a second game. The system was. And I'm being used to ensure of Maryland as it was anywhere in the country. And that includes the inter-pacity of the night. Yeah, yeah. Did you go to the right in turn there? Did you guys have a relationship? That was an all-star game that was born. One thing here, and the old memorials stated where the civic center is now. They had a memorial stadium. They had lights. And they played an all-star game. All stars from all of these black teams around. They played against the all-star's memorial. And that was something really big thing for us. You know, the Nazily number one player out of the night. We only do in two places, we're unsure where we play on the night and the night. That program of it can about a half a dozen flashlights up on the poles. And we just go down and play. We chased a many account, the fly, many birds, thinking it was the ball. And down in Atomac, Virginia, that little place called Kim and the Society of America, Kim and the head are light. Now in Delaware, up around Millford, there in New Luminca. Now they had very good facilities. Millford had excellent facilities. They had lights and everything there. And we used to like to go and play them. The team called the Millford Yanks, living there. They were probably just a step under the Negro League. The team that came in the Negro League, living there. They were very charming for us. And we measured how well we could play by what we did against that. They were very, very good. Three years ago, I saw a guy over in Millford. And I was just amazed that the females, they made my fields look like counting. I was amazed. They were very proud to be out there. I was amazed. They were very good. They were very good. They had great fields, expenses, and signs and everything. To me, you know, people playing with these baseball people. But those people, Millford and Roses, they didn't just start. And they played a very good brand of baseball. Now, you know, they had the term hardball. We played for the most part hardball. And they were places like the Millford and Roses. And we have another team called the Susser to the Jigs out there. A couple of other teams. We learned what baseball is all about. And we can come back and try to do some of the things that we saw. But as far as formal training camps and that kind of thing, we played hardball really, rather than a good brand of baseball, we just played on a natural talent. And we had a good tournament of all two of my goals. They were the pitchers, you know. A lot of the other teams. And it's kind of like, I didn't say it. But today, there's so many of these camps with them. Like that growing up, I grew up moving out and playing baseball. And then moving out and moving basketball, season came around, playing football. And now, everybody has some computer. They get the game and Xbox or things of that sort of place. And then successes play coaching. Well, I'm very proud of that. The biggest thing we're growing up, as I said, was playing on the All-Star team. The other thing was making the college team the little country boy. And from the community teams, going to a place like North Carolina and T, they're growing up for the ball club when they had guys that were regularly drafted into the post. When I went there, they had just given the guy $100,000 to sign. There were other guys on the team that were looking for contracts. They had catchers that signed contracts. After I closed my mouth from being in all these guys and settled down and down out there, it was hitting the ball when it's thrown to you, kissing it when it's thrown to you, that kind of thing. To make that ball club, that was really one of the highlights that one of the best things that ever happened. There are two years for it. Baseball was concerned. And then to have played four years at ENT. And that was highlight there. And of course, when I saw the talent and everything, I thought that the oscillators had guys that could play ball, like Sam Dohn and Williams to it as a picture. There are some of the other guys there. One guy that probably should have been the whole thing there and then didn't do it. He means Charles to it. He was in the... He had worked in Habitat, who was now the labor as per silence. Chipson is a big but only. It's maybe once a month. If he pitched a ball game, he would have to give his arm a few breaths. He hits for us, say, on the fourth and two laps. He was no more good in the leg. But he was probably one of the best pictures that we've played. But then when I went to ENT, and I saw guys 17, 18, 19 years old, throwing up with twice as fast, with curveball breaking, you know, much better than anything else as well. And I soon realized that it was a tremendous step from where I stood to bang any kind of professional ball. It seemed like, you know, over the years, you know, something that I've coached. You know, first of all, it's about $0.00. And they were the first thing you know about it. So pictures are different. They could better control. And they have a little faster. They said that they'd throw that slider in about eight to five miles. You've never seen that in Nashville. That slider is the difference. You know, because they think it's an sharp hurt. I guess the scene for walks and the old building those are the local things, right? You know, they have, you know, yeah, some pictures on the wall over there, the new stadium. They don't say that there was a local team. It was probably as good a local team as they were around here. We always thought that we've played, at least a ball. And some of our guys probably would have been successful and double a ball. But we always prided ourselves as being good enough to hold our home in a ball. Yeah. They won't picture there with George and Jesse Lucas or Stuart and yourself. With that championship team, with those pictures of Sam Donne donated that picture there. And I would have to see that picture to know I could have brought you a picture of some of these guys there. But George, I guess what? 70 in the 70s. We formed a league. I'm kind of trying to take a name out of it. We formed a league. There were one that league every year. It was still segregated. And I don't know that there was any integration until recently, maybe in the 80s. Well, there. Well, the team went together. There. I think maybe Krupp and Paul was probably and he was an old guy then, playing with one of the teams there. Wow. After all, our guys got the control for baseball and softball. Yeah. It's like that. The teams played in segregated systems throughout the until probably the latter part of the 70s the game is there. But by that time, community ball clubs were flying up. Guys who could play and hold on teams were moving out of the area, following the jobs and everything. A lot of them were going to college and everything. They were not hanging around. And the team started closing up because of it. It became difficult to find teams to play. Those were the ones that was one of the last teams to fall. I came to Seoul very in 1976. I was very nervous. I was very surprised to see Paul fall. And of course, you know they all stood about me. I got stuck coaching them as two years didn't have a goal. And the other thing I heard when I came to Seoul very was about the fastball football with Joe Powell and Chubby Jones and Gladden and people like that. And the gym didn't get involved in fastball football. And the first thing is, it seems like us old baseball players going to like football. Well, we did not go into fastball for a primary because we did not have a picture. We went into a more than called modified softball because we could compete. And of course, you know, we were very, very careful not to get into anything which we could not compete, especially after things were integrated. There might have been integration, but our minds were not integrated. Our minds were still such that we wanted to prove that we should have been integrated long-term goal that we were just as good. So if we, so that we could not compete, then we didn't get into it. And fast pitch, you know, softball is what, 90, 95 percent, and we would rather play snow pitch. And then the field was at home. But the modified we could compete. And to update the modified softball, can I be 60? You know. Do we take breakers?
